cover.kicker: Finland · India · Maldives · Sri Lanka · Hong Kong
cover.route: Dallas · Helsinki · Delhi · Agra · Varanasi · Mumbai · Maldives · Sri Lanka · Hong Kong · Dallas
cover.blurb: Thirty-five days, six countries, and a quarter-century of marriage celebrated from a Baltic sauna to a Ganges sunrise to a leopard's dawn in the Sri Lankan scrub.

itinerary.move: Peradeniya · Temple of the Tooth puja

flights.detail: Cathay Pacific CX876 · Premium Economy · Jul 11

ground.detail: Vande Bharat Express 20176, Executive Class · Jun 11, 6:00am

lines: Land Helsinki-Vantaa 10:45am. Immigration is efficient, roughly 30 minutes
Airport Express Ring Rail to Helsinki Central Station — 30 minutes, €5.50pp, every 10 minutes
Hotel U14 is steps from Market Square — ask about early check-in, officially 3pm
CX612 departs Colombo 00:50 — a midnight flight, so the whole of Jul 8 belongs to you
Allow a minimum of 3 hours before international departure, plus a buffer for Colombo traffic
Dinner at Ministry of Crab, then straight to CMB — no hotel needed for the short overnight

days.title: Helsinki
days.intro: The journey begins with a long Nordic afternoon: a fortress island in the harbour, a wood-fired sauna built like a rock on the shoreline, and a tasting menu built entirely from what Finland itself can grow, forage, or pull from the sea. By the time the plane to Delhi leaves, you will already feel like you have been somewhere.

items.name: Market Square (Kauppatori)
items.desc: Waterfront stalls for smoked salmon, fried vendace, and fresh crayfish, with harbour ferries coming and going all afternoon.

items.name: Suomenlinna Sea Fortress
items.desc: An 18th-century island fortress reached by a 15-minute public ferry — stone ramparts, sea bastions, and roughly 800 year-round residents.

items.name: Löyly Sauna
items.desc: A faceted wooden sauna house on the Hernesaari shoreline. Traditional wood-burning and smoke saunas, both with direct access to a cold-plunge into the Baltic.

items.name: Design District
items.desc: Marimekko and Iittala flagships on Esplanadi, a short walk from the Temppeliaukio Rock Church carved into granite bedrock.

lodging.name: Hotel U14, Autograph Collection
lodging.desc: A Marriott Bonvoy property on Unioninkatu, a few steps from Market Square and the Esplanadi.

restaurants.name: Ravintola Skörd
restaurants.desc: A seven-course tasting menu built entirely from Finnish ingredients — no pepper, no avocado, nothing from outside Finland's borders except salt. Paired with Finnish berry wines.

restaurants.name: Café Ekberg
restaurants.desc: Helsinki's oldest café, established 1852. Famous for cardamom rolls and rye bread.

notes.text: Swimwear is required at all times in the Löyly saunas — mixed sessions, separate changing rooms.
In June, Helsinki barely gets dark. The harbour and esplanade at midnight in soft twilight is one of the city's quiet pleasures.
Marimekko and Iittala goods generally run 20–30% cheaper here than US retail pricing.
Order a Finnish berry wine with dinner — cloudberry and lingonberry both appear on tasting menus in June.
Suomenlinna's stone ramparts at the island's seaward edge frame the harbour and skyline well in the long, low evening light of June.

days.title: Agra
days.tagline: Marble that changes colour with the sky
days.intro: A pre-dawn landing in Delhi and a smooth drive down the Yamuna Expressway puts you in Agra by mid-morning. The afternoon belongs entirely to the Taj Mahal — first up close, then from across the river as the light turns gold.

items.name: Taj Mahal
items.desc: Foreign-tourist entry, with a licensed guide hired at the gate well worth the cost for the architectural history. Allow 1.5–2 hours for the mausoleum, mosque, and gardens.

items.name: Mehtab Bagh
items.desc: A Mughal garden directly across the Yamuna River from the Taj. At sunset the reflection in the river is one of India's classic photographs, with far fewer crowds.

lodging.name: ITC Mughal, A Luxury Collection Resort & Spa
lodging.desc: Mughal-inspired gardens and multiple restaurants, including the celebrated Peshawri, positioned perfectly for an early Taj Mahal visit the following morning.

restaurants.name: Peshawri
restaurants.desc: Frontier and Mughal cuisine inside the ITC Mughal — the dal bukhara, slow-cooked 18 hours, is one of the best meals of the trip.

restaurants.name: Pinch of Spice
restaurants.desc: A local favourite for Mughal cuisine, Agra petha sweets, and biryani between sightseeing.

notes.text: The Taj Mahal is closed every Friday — not an issue on a Wednesday visit, but worth checking for any schedule change.
Bedai with aloo sabzi, sold by street vendors near Sadar Bazaar, is the traditional Agra breakfast.
Sunrise over the Yamuna River plains, seen from the expressway, is some of the flattest, most extraordinary light in India.
The Taj's marble shifts colour through the day — pale pink at dawn, blinding white at midday, gold at sunset — so a return visit at a different hour is worth the second entry fee.

days.title: Varanasi
days.epithet: The oldest living city on the Ganges
days.tagline: Fire, water, and five thousand years of continuous habitation
days.intro: The Vande Bharat Express carries you east through the Gangetic plains to the holiest city in India. Three and a half nights on the ghats means dawn boat rides, the nightly Ganga Aarti fire ceremony, and the narrow lanes of the old city, all built around a river that has never stopped moving.

items.name: Dashashwamedh Ghat
items.desc: Varanasi's main ghat, where the Ganga Aarti ceremony unfolds every evening — dozens of priests, fire, conch shells, and chanting watched by hundreds.

items.name: Dawn boat ride from Munshi Ghat
items.desc: A private rowing boat before sunrise, gliding past pilgrims bathing and temples glowing in the first light — among the most memorable two hours in India.

items.name: Sarnath
items.desc: Eleven kilometres from Varanasi, where the Buddha gave his first sermon. The Dhamek Stupa marks the spot, alongside a museum holding the original Lion Capital of Ashoka.

items.name: Old city heritage walk
items.desc: Narrow galis lined with temples, chai stalls, and silk workshops — the layered chaos of thousands of years of continuous habitation.

items.name: Manikarnika Ghat
items.desc: One of the city's two cremation ghats, considered the most auspicious place in India to be cremated — observed respectfully from a distance, never photographed.

items.name: Ramnagar Fort
items.desc: An 18th-century sandstone fort across the Ganges, seat of the former Maharaja of Benares, with a museum of vintage cars, royal palanquins, and antique weaponry.

lodging.name: BrijRama Palace
lodging.desc: Built directly into the ghats at Munshi Ghat, with a private terrace above the river itself and resident teachers for dawn meditation sessions. The building began life as an 18th-century riverside palace and stood derelict for decades before a meticulous restoration reopened it as a heritage hotel — the only luxury property built directly into Varanasi's ghats.

restaurants.name: Baati Chokha
restaurants.desc: The definitive Varanasi meal — claypot dal, baked wheat baati, and roasted vegetable chokha.

restaurants.name: Kashi Chaat Bhandar
restaurants.desc: Legendary street food — tamatar chaat with sour tamarind, eaten standing at the counter.

restaurants.name: Lassi shops near Dashashwamedh Ghat
restaurants.desc: Thick, cold lassi served in clay cups — Varanasi's most iconic drink.

restaurants.name: Pizzeria Vatika Café
restaurants.desc: A riverside terrace at Assi Ghat for fresh juice, wood-fired pizza, and a break from spice between temple visits.

notes.text: Manikarnika Ghat is a working cremation ground. Photography is not permitted — observe respectfully from a distance.
A private guided meditation at dawn on the ghat terrace, arranged through the hotel, is one of the most significant experiences of the whole trip.
Banarasi silk weavers, arranged through the hotel, are a reputable source for genuine local textiles — the weave dates back over a thousand years and remains a city-defining craft.
Varanasi is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on earth, with settlement dating back roughly 3,000 years.

days.title: Maldives
days.tagline: House reef, dolphins, and the widest sky on the trip
days.intro: Four nights on Vommuli Island, with an overwater villa whose deck steps directly into the lagoon. June is peak season for whale sharks and spinner dolphins, and the equatorial night sky here has essentially zero light pollution.

items.name: House reef snorkelling
items.desc: Blacktip reef sharks, sea turtles, and manta rays directly below the overwater villas, best in early morning light.

items.name: Dolphin sunset cruise
items.desc: Spinner dolphins are prolific in Maldivian waters in June, often leaping at the bow as the sun sets — a fast shutter speed rewards patience as they leap at the bow.

items.name: St. Regis Spa
items.desc: Underwater-inspired treatment rooms and traditional Maldivian healing therapies, including couple's massage.

items.name: Manta point excursion
items.desc: A short boat ride to a known cleaning station where reef manta rays circle in shallow, clear water — one of the more reliable big-animal photography opportunities in the atoll.

lodging.name: St. Regis Maldives Vommuli Resort
lodging.desc: An overwater villa resort on Vommuli Island in Dhaalu Atoll, reached by speedboat or seaplane transfer from Malé. The island sat undeveloped until the resort's 2018 opening, and its overwater villas were built on stilts driven directly into the lagoon bed rather than on reclaimed land, leaving the reef below largely undisturbed.

wineries.name: Reef manta ray, manta point
wineries.desc: Best photographed from above at the cleaning station in calm, shallow water with good overhead light.

wineries.name: White-tern & frigatebird, offshore
wineries.desc: Frigatebirds often trail the dolphin cruise boat, gliding low over the wake.

restaurants.name: Orientale
restaurants.desc: Fine dining with Indian Ocean views — Maldivian tuna, lobster, and coconut-based dishes. Reserve a sunset table.

restaurants.name: Whale Bar
restaurants.desc: An overwater bar at the island's tip, the best sunset spot on the property.

restaurants.name: Alba
restaurants.desc: Mediterranean-leaning seafood and salads on an open deck above the lagoon — a lighter counterpoint to the evening tasting menus.

notes.text: A private beach dinner is arranged for the second evening, set directly on the sand with lanterns and an Indian Ocean backdrop.
Ask for mas huni at breakfast — shredded smoked tuna with coconut and chilli, served with fresh roshi flatbread.
The Maldives sits on the equator with essentially no light pollution — the Milky Way is visible from the villa deck on clear nights.
The villa deck's overwater steps make for one of the best blue-hour reflection shots of the trip, roughly twenty minutes after sunset.

days.title: Trincomalee
days.epithet: Whales, reefs, and a lagoon at the door
days.tagline: East coast Sri Lanka in its calmest season
days.intro: A short flight and a long drive bring you into Sri Lanka proper, arriving late at a lagoon-set resort outside Trincomalee. June is peak whale-watching season on the east coast, and the beaches here are among the finest in the country.

items.name: Whale watching, Trincomalee Harbour
items.desc: Blue whales, sperm whales, and spinner dolphins are common in June, with sea turtles and seabirds offshore — a fast telephoto and a stabilised deck position give the best shot at a breach.

items.name: Pigeon Island National Park
items.desc: Sri Lanka's best snorkelling — blacktip reef sharks, sea turtles, and colourful coral in excellent June visibility.

items.name: Koneswaram Temple
items.desc: A dramatic Hindu temple on a clifftop above the ocean, dating to at least the 3rd century BC, best visited at sunset.

items.name: Fort Frederick
items.desc: A Dutch and British colonial fort, still garrisoned, known for the spotted deer that wander freely through its grounds — tame enough for close, unhurried portraits in the late-afternoon light.

items.name: Kanniya Hot Springs
items.desc: Seven ancient natural spring wells, each a different temperature — a unique geological curiosity.

lodging.name: Amaranthé Bay Resort & Spa
lodging.desc: A boutique resort built within a palm grove at the edge of the Pillaikulam Aru lagoon — quieter and more private than a beachfront stay, with abundant birdlife visible from the room. The lagoon frontage was once part of a coconut estate, and many of the original palms were preserved and built around rather than cleared during construction.

wineries.name: Brown Booby & seabirds, offshore
wineries.desc: Spotted on the whale-watching boat, alongside Brahminy Kite and Little Tern along the nesting beaches.

wineries.name: Spot-billed Pelican, Trinco Harbour
wineries.desc: Common around the harbour, with Painted Stork and Indian Pond Heron in the coastal lagoons.

wineries.name: Spotted deer, Fort Frederick
wineries.desc: Habituated to visitors and easy to photograph at close range against the fort's colonial ramparts.

restaurants.name: ISSO Trincomalee
restaurants.desc: The best seafood in town — a seafood platter for two, grilled whole fish, and calamari curry straight off the local boats.

restaurants.name: Café on the 18th, Uppuveli
restaurants.desc: Banana porridge, overnight oats, and coconut rotti — good pre-whale-watching fuel.

restaurants.name: Dutch Bank Café
restaurants.desc: Famous for kottu roti, Trincomalee's most iconic street food.

notes.text: Book the Junior Suite with private jacuzzi if possible — bay windows over the lagoon make it one of the best rooms in Sri Lanka.
Marble Beach is air-force controlled, very clean, and largely missed by other tourists — good for an uncrowded wide shot of the bay.
The lagoon setting at the resort produces excellent birding directly from the room balcony at dawn.

days.title: Somawathiya & Sigiriya
days.epithet: Elephants, an ancient stupa, and a cave temple
days.tagline: The day the journey turns inland
days.intro: A dawn safari through Somawathiya's floodplains — home to the largest elephant concentration in Sri Lanka — gives way to an afternoon drive west, with a stop at the rock-cut Dambulla Cave Temple en route to four nights based in the Cultural Triangle.

items.name: Somawathiya National Park
items.desc: Vast floodplains of the Mahaweli River with around 400 elephants in the park and surrounds, plus deer, jackals, and crocodiles at the shrinking dry-season water basins.

items.name: Somawathiya Chaitya
items.desc: A 2,200-year-old Buddhist stupa on the riverbank, said to enshrine a tooth relic of the Buddha — sacred ground inside a wildlife park, unusual anywhere in Asia.

items.name: Dambulla Cave Temple
items.desc: A UNESCO site with 150 Buddha statues across five cave complexes, reached by a barefoot climb of roughly 200 steps.

lodging.name: Jetwing Vil Uyana
lodging.desc: Thatched cabanas built over a wetland lake with an infinity pool, and exceptional birding directly from the deck — the base for four nights in the Cultural Triangle.

wineries.name: Sri Lanka Junglefowl, Somawathiya
wineries.desc: The endemic national bird, alongside Painted Stork and Spot-billed Pelican across the villus.

wineries.name: Black-necked Stork, Somawathiya
wineries.desc: Rare and elusive — Somawathiya is one of the best locations in the country to find one.

restaurants.name: Jetwing Vil Uyana dining room
restaurants.desc: A traditional rice-and-curry buffet, eight to twelve small curries — always ask for the local menu.

notes.text: Rusty-spotted cat, fishing cat, and leopard are all rare but possible sightings at Somawathiya.
Dress modestly for the Dambulla cave climb — shoes come off at the entrance.
The Jetwing Vil Uyana wetland at dusk is excellent for waterbirds, kingfishers, and raptors without leaving the hotel grounds.

days.title: Anuradhapura
days.epithet: The tree that was old when Rome was new
days.tagline: Bo Tree at dawn, Pidurangala at dusk
days.intro: Sri Lanka's first ancient capital holds the oldest documented tree on earth, a trio of colossal stupas, and some of the finest stone carving anywhere in Asia. The day ends back at Sigiriya, climbing Pidurangala Rock for the definitive view across the jungle to Sigiriya's vertical face.

items.name: Sri Maha Bodhi
items.desc: The oldest living tree with a documented planting date, 288 BC, grown from a cutting of the original Bodhi Tree in Bodh Gaya. White or light clothing expected of all visitors.

items.name: Ruwanwelisaya Dagoba
items.desc: A 103-metre white dome built 140 BC — its scale only becomes apparent walking the full circumference.

items.name: Thuparamaya
items.desc: Sri Lanka's oldest stupa, built 247 BC, said to enshrine the collarbone relic of the Buddha.

items.name: Twin Ponds (Kuttam Pokuna)
items.desc: 8th-century royal bathing pools of extraordinary engineering precision, still perfectly intact.

items.name: Pidurangala Rock
items.desc: A 30–45 minute jungle climb to a summit facing Sigiriya Rock directly — the definitive wide-angle view of Sigiriya, unavailable from the rock itself.

lodging.name: Jetwing Vil Uyana
lodging.desc: Second night of four at the wetland-lake property near Sigiriya.

wineries.name: Indian Peafowl, Anuradhapura
wineries.desc: In full breeding plumage in June, with spectacular displays among the ancient stonework.

wineries.name: Crested Serpent Eagle
wineries.desc: Commonly seen soaring over the scrub around the ancient city sites.

restaurants.name: Local rice and curry, Anuradhapura town
restaurants.desc: A simple, fresh local meal between ancient-city sites — ask the driver for a recommendation.

notes.text: Shoes come off at the Bo Tree — move slowly, it is one of the most sacred objects in the Buddhist world.
Mihintale, 12km away, is where Buddhism first arrived in Sri Lanka in 247 BC and can be added to this day with an earlier 6am departure.
Bring a wide-angle lens for the Pidurangala summit — the ancient gardens and moats lie out geometrically below in golden light.

days.title: Sigiriya Dawn & The Gathering
days.epithet: Two of Asia's great wildlife mornings in one day
days.tagline: A 200-metre rock at dawn, hundreds of elephants by dusk
days.intro: First light brings the climb up Sigiriya Rock itself — the Lion's Paws, the 8th-century Mirror Wall graffiti, and the only surviving secular ancient frescoes in Sri Lanka. After a rest through the heat of the day, the afternoon belongs to Minneriya, where hundreds of wild elephants gather at the reservoir as the dry season tightens its grip.

items.name: Sigiriya Rock
items.desc: A 200-metre vertical climb past the Lion's Paws platform, the Mirror Wall poetry, and the Cloud Maidens frescoes, to 5th-century palace ruins at the summit.

items.name: Minneriya National Park
items.desc: Between June and October, hundreds of wild elephants converge on the tank as seasonal water sources dry up — one of the world's great wildlife spectacles, best at 4:30–6pm golden hour.

wineries.name: Elephant Gathering, Minneriya Tank
wineries.desc: Up to 400 wild Asian elephants at the water's edge in golden-hour light, alongside Painted Stork and Spot-billed Pelican colonies.

wineries.name: Malabar Pied Hornbill, Sigiriya area
wineries.desc: Found in fruiting trees around the rock and surrounding forest.

restaurants.name: Roadside rice and curry, Habarana
restaurants.desc: Ask the driver for the local workers' lunch spot — invariably the best food of the day, for under two dollars.

notes.text: Be at the Sigiriya gates as they open, ideally among the first through, to descend before the heat builds and the tour groups arrive.
Kaudulla National Park is adjacent to Minneriya and elephant herds move between them — ask the driver which park has the better concentration that day.
A half-day Sri Lankan cooking class near Habarana — rice and curry, pol sambol, hoppers from scratch — is a memorable way to spend a quieter evening at this base.

days.title: Polonnaruwa
days.tagline: Sri Lanka's second great ancient city, far fewer crowds
days.intro: Polonnaruwa was Sri Lanka's medieval capital from the 11th to 13th centuries — more compact than Anuradhapura and far less visited, and best explored slowly by rented bicycle through ancient parkland.

items.name: Gal Vihara
items.desc: Four colossal Buddha figures carved into a single granite cliff face, including a 15-metre reclining figure — among the masterpieces of Buddhist art in Asia.

items.name: Royal Palace of Parakramabahu
items.desc: Seven-storey palace ruins whose elephant-frieze audience hall is considered the finest ancient stone carving in Sri Lanka.

items.name: The Vatadage
items.desc: A circular relic house with four moonstone-guarded entrances, possibly the most perfectly preserved ancient building in the country.

items.name: Lotus Pond (Nelum Pokuna)
items.desc: An octagonal bathing pool of extraordinary geometric precision, completely intact after eight centuries.

lodging.name: Jetwing Vil Uyana
lodging.desc: Fourth and final night at this base.

wineries.name: Sri Lanka Junglefowl, among the ruins
wineries.desc: Endemic and abundant around the ancient stonework, alongside Painted Stork at the Parakrama Samudra reservoir.

restaurants.name: Local restaurant, Polonnaruwa town
restaurants.desc: Simple rice and curry at the market area — ask the driver for a recommendation.

notes.text: Bicycles can be rented for a few dollars at the entrance gate — the entire site is flat and easily cyclable, roughly a six-kilometre circuit.
Arrive before the tour groups for cool morning air and quiet light across the jungle and ruins.

days.title: Kandy
days.epithet: The cultural capital, in one evening
days.tagline: Botanical gardens by day, sacred drums by night
days.intro: A short drive south from the Cultural Triangle, with an optional stop at a spice garden en route, brings you to Kandy in time for the Royal Botanical Gardens, a walk around the lake, and the evening puja at the Temple of the Sacred Tooth Relic.

items.name: Royal Botanical Gardens, Peradeniya
items.desc: 147 acres including an enormous orchid collection, a giant rubber tree planted in 1828, and an avenue of royal palms.

items.name: Kandy Lake
items.desc: The city's centrepiece, ringed by its iconic cloud wall, with the temple complex reflected in the water.

items.name: Sri Dalada Maligawa (Temple of the Tooth)
items.desc: Evening puja with traditional Kandyan drumming and ritual offerings to the most spiritually significant relic in Sri Lanka. Arrive 30 minutes early for a good position; shoes off, modest dress.

lodging.name: The Radh Hotel
lodging.desc: A boutique property steps from the Temple of the Tooth and close to the railway station, ideal for the early train to Nuwara Eliya the next morning.

wineries.name: Sri Lanka Grey Hornbill, Peradeniya
wineries.desc: Endemic and common in the botanical gardens, alongside the endemic Sri Lanka Hanging Parrot and Layard's Parakeet.

restaurants.name: Kandyan Muslim Hotel
restaurants.desc: Plate-sized naan, beef masala, and the best kottu roti in Kandy — very cheap and beloved by locals.

restaurants.name: Empire Café
restaurants.desc: Coconut rotti with lunu miris, beside the Temple of the Tooth.

notes.text: Kandy's old market and gem shops reflect Sri Lanka's status as the world's third-largest gemstone exporter.
Confirm the morning train details with the driver before bed — departure to Nuwara Eliya is early.
Kandy Lake's still morning water gives the cleanest reflection of the temple complex and cloud-wall battlements, before the wind picks up later in the day.

days.title: Nuwara Eliya
days.tagline: Tea estates, mist, and a 130-year-old high tea
days.intro: The climb into tea country brings the first cool evening of the trip — emerald plantation rows, a working tea factory tour, and high tea at a colonial institution established in 1891.

items.name: Pedro Estate / Mackwoods Labookellie
items.desc: A full walk through the tea process — plucking, withering, rolling, fermenting, drying — with a tasting session included.

items.name: Gregory Lake
items.desc: A pleasant waterfront walk with good late-afternoon light.

items.name: The Grand Hotel
items.desc: High tea in full Edwardian splendour — scones, finger sandwiches, and Ceylon tea, established 1891. Book in advance.

lodging.name: Heritance Tea Factory
lodging.desc: The highest hotel in Sri Lanka at 6,850 feet, a converted Victorian tea factory on the Hethersett Estate. The Executive Floor gives the best views over the misty plantation.

wineries.name: Sri Lanka Blue Magpie
wineries.desc: An endemic priority species for the hill country, alongside the endemic Sri Lanka Wood Pigeon and Sri Lanka White-eye.

restaurants.name: The Grand Hotel High Tea
restaurants.desc: The most colonial experience in Sri Lanka — English high tea in a 130-year-old hotel at 6,000 feet. Book ahead.

restaurants.name: De Zoysa's Restaurant
restaurants.desc: Excellent local rice and curry, the highland version with more root vegetables and less coconut than the coast.

notes.text: A tea-picking experience can be booked directly with the hotel on arrival — traditional dress, plucking in the estate fields, then a tour of the mini factory.
Evenings here run 14–18°C — the first genuinely cool night of the whole trip. Pack a layer.
Morning mist often sits low over the plantation rows below the Executive Floor — worth a five-minute look before breakfast even on a tight schedule.

days.title: Ella
days.epithet: One of Asia's great train journeys, then three days on foot
days.tagline: Nine Arch Bridge, ridge hikes, and curd with treacle
days.intro: The descent from Nuwara Eliya by rail is one of the most photogenic train journeys anywhere — rolling tea estates, the Demodara Loop spiralling inside a mountain, and a final approach into one of the most charming small stations in the world. Three nights here are spent largely on foot, between sunrise hikes and the endlessly photogenic Nine Arch Bridge.

items.name: Nine Arch Bridge
items.desc: A colonial-era viaduct from 1921 spanning a jungle ravine — time a visit for a train crossing, roughly every two to three hours, for the definitive shot.

items.name: Little Adam's Peak
items.desc: A moderate 45-minute sunrise climb to 1,141m with panoramic views across Ella Gap and the tea estates.

items.name: Ella Rock
items.desc: A more demanding 3–4 hour return hike to 1,400m, wilder and quieter than Little Adam's Peak, with good forest birding.

items.name: Ravana Falls
items.desc: One of Sri Lanka's widest waterfalls at 25 metres, with swimming possible in the pool below and a connection to the Ramayana epic.

items.name: Demodara Nine Arch & Loop
items.desc: The railway spirals through a tunnel beneath itself to lose altitude — visible from a roadside viewpoint above the loop, a different angle from the bridge itself.

items.name: Halpewatte Tea Factory
items.desc: A working tea factory open for short tours of the withering, rolling, and drying floors, ending with a tasting overlooking the estate.

lodging.name: 98 Acres Resort & Spa
lodging.desc: Set along a tea-estate ridge with panoramic views over Ella Gap and an infinity pool — the best photography base in town. The property is built on a working tea estate of the same name, and several of the original tea bushes remain planted around the grounds and walking paths.

wineries.name: Sri Lanka Frogmouth
wineries.desc: A nocturnal endemic and one of the most sought species in Sri Lanka — ask the guesthouse about local guide and roost locations.

wineries.name: White-faced Starling
wineries.desc: Endemic to the forest canopy, alongside the Pompadour Green Pigeon along fig-lined trails.

wineries.name: Sri Lanka Whistling Thrush
wineries.desc: A shy, endemic forest dweller often heard before it is seen along the Ella Rock trail.

restaurants.name: Matey Hut
restaurants.desc: Home-cooked Sri Lankan food — the jackfruit curry and pol sambol are particularly good.

restaurants.name: The Down Town Roti Hut
restaurants.desc: Famous kottu roti with a line of locals waiting — standing at the counter is part of the experience.

restaurants.name: Curd and treacle vendors
restaurants.desc: Buffalo curd topped with kithul palm treacle — the hill country's signature dessert, for under a dollar.

restaurants.name: Cafe Chill
restaurants.desc: A relaxed garden restaurant on the main strip with Ella Gap views — good wood-fired pizza and fresh lime soda after a hike.

notes.text: Sit on the right side of the carriage from Nuwara Eliya for the best view of the Nine Arch Bridge approach.
Visit the bridge at two different times of day — the light quality changes substantially between morning and afternoon train crossings.
The viewpoint above the Demodara Loop gives a wide aerial angle on the spiraling track that the bridge itself cannot offer.

days.title: Arugam Bay & Kumana
days.tagline: From hill country to dry-zone surf town in two hours
days.intro: A dramatic descent from the highlands lands you on the laid-back east coast, where a world-famous surf break sits a short walk from a mangrove lagoon and one of Sri Lanka's least-visited national parks.

items.name: Pottuvil Lagoon boat safari
items.desc: A mangrove ecosystem where wild elephants visit the water's edge and crocodiles bask on the banks, with a 2,000-year-old partially submerged temple as a bonus stop.

items.name: Arugam Bay Main Point
items.desc: A world-famous right-hand surf break, spectacular in golden-hour light even if you are only watching.

items.name: Kumana National Park
items.desc: Yala's wilder, quieter twin, with far fewer vehicles. June–July is peak season for migratory waterbirds across its twenty lagoons, and leopard density some say exceeds Yala's.

items.name: Elephant Rock
items.desc: A short walk from town to a granite outcrop shaped like a resting elephant, with sweeping views back over the bay — a classic late-afternoon vantage point.

lodging.name: Hideaway Resort
lodging.desc: An AC Heritage Room on Main Point Road, rated among the highest in the area, with breakfast included. The property grew out of a small family-run guesthouse during the town's early surf-tourism years and still keeps that low-key, garden-style layout rather than a conventional hotel block.

wineries.name: Black-necked Stork & Painted Stork, Kumana
wineries.desc: Peak season for tens of thousands of migratory waterbirds across Kumana's lagoons, alongside Eurasian Spoonbill.

wineries.name: Kudumbigala Monastery
wineries.desc: A 2,000-year-old cave monastery on a rocky outcrop inside the park, still home to resident monks.

wineries.name: Wild elephant, Pottuvil Lagoon
wineries.desc: Elephants come to drink at the mangrove's edge — the boat safari gives a low, eye-level angle rarely possible from a jeep.

wineries.name: Sri Lankan leopard, Kumana
wineries.desc: Lower vehicle density than Yala means sightings are quieter and less crowded when they happen.

restaurants.name: Mama's Rice & Curry
restaurants.desc: An all-you-can-eat local curry buffet, possibly the best value rice and curry in Sri Lanka, for under two dollars.

restaurants.name: Salty Swamis
restaurants.desc: The best garden restaurant in town — ocean views from canopy beds and excellent fresh seafood.

restaurants.name: Stardust Beach Club
restaurants.desc: Beachfront seating and wood-fired pizza, popular with the surf crowd at sunset.

notes.text: Book the Kumana safari as a private full-day jeep through the hotel — departure is around 5am for the 35-kilometre drive south.
Kumana is directly connected to Yala's ecosystem but draws far fewer vehicles even at peak times.
Elephant Rock at sunset gives an elevated, uncrowded panorama of the bay and surf break together.

days.title: Yala
days.epithet: The world's highest density of leopards
days.tagline: An anniversary day in a curved tented pod between jungle and ocean
days.intro: Two nights at an all-inclusive Relais & Châteaux lodge, with a dawn safari included for each night stayed. The 25th-anniversary day itself falls here — a morning safari, a private plunge pool, and a lantern-lit dinner with the sound of the sea.

items.name: Yala Block 1 / Block 5 safari
items.desc: Dawn entry for the best leopard sighting chances before the heat pushes the cats into shade. Also sloth bear, elephant, crocodile, and over 215 bird species.

items.name: Patanangala Rock
items.desc: A wave-smoothed beach boulder inside Block 1 where the jungle meets the open Indian Ocean — a rare combined coast-and-jungle frame, and a good stop on the return leg of a safari.

lodging.name: Wild Coast Tented Lodge
lodging.desc: The Cocoon Suite — a curved tented pod between the jungle and the Indian Ocean with a private plunge pool, a UNESCO Design Award winner and Michelin 2-Key property. All meals, drinks, and activities included. The lodge's cocoon-shaped tents were designed to echo a spider-web silhouette in the surrounding jungle canopy, and each was built off-grid on a raised timber platform to minimise disturbance to the land beneath it.

wineries.name: Leopard, Yala Block 1
wineries.desc: The world's highest density of leopards anywhere — a 400mm+ lens and a fast shutter reward patience.

wineries.name: Sri Lanka Junglefowl & Indian Roller
wineries.desc: Both very common throughout, alongside Malabar Pied Hornbill in the fruiting trees.

wineries.name: Sloth bear, Block 1
wineries.desc: Less predictable than the leopards but a prized sighting — most often found digging at termite mounds near scrub edges.

restaurants.name: Wild Coast Tented Lodge dining deck
restaurants.desc: An open-sided dining pavilion above the jungle, included in the all-inclusive rate — ask for a lantern-lit table on the anniversary evening.

notes.text: Both dawn safaris are already covered by the booking — simply arrange timing with the lodge on arrival.
Leopard, elephant, and sloth bear have all been recorded moving through the camp at night.
Patanangala Rock at the end of a morning drive offers a rare frame combining open jungle and ocean horizon in one shot.

days.title: Udawalawe
days.epithet: Five hundred elephants, wide open ground
days.tagline: Sri Lanka's most reliable elephant park
days.intro: A short drive west from Yala leads to a park known above all for elephants — over 500 free-roaming individuals across open terrain that makes them easy to find and photograph in golden morning light.

items.name: Udawalawe morning safari
items.desc: Open terrain and a large resident elephant population make this the most reliable park in Sri Lanka for elephant sightings, especially around the reservoir at dawn.

items.name: Elephant Transit Home
items.desc: An orphaned elephant rehabilitation centre half a kilometre from the park entrance, with public feeding times through the day. No riding — a genuine conservation facility.

lodging.name: Centauria Wild Resort
lodging.desc: On Thanamalwila Road near the park entrance, with an early 10am check-out that pairs well with an afternoon-of-arrival safari.

wineries.name: Elephant herds, open grassland
wineries.desc: Telephoto essential — over 500 free-roaming elephants make this the park's signature sighting.

wineries.name: Lesser Adjutant Stork & Changeable Hawk-Eagle
wineries.desc: Both regularly seen at the reservoir and forest edge.

notes.text: Plan the safari for the afternoon of arrival to leave the next morning free for an easy departure to Galle, given the early check-out.
Peacock displays are at their most spectacular here in June's breeding season — worth the patience.

days.title: Galle Fort
days.epithet: The last Sri Lankan evening, inside Dutch colonial walls
days.tagline: Tailoring, ramparts, and a farewell crab dinner
days.intro: Two nights inside the fort walls give time for a proper farewell to Sri Lanka — tailoring fitted and collected same-day, a full circuit of the ramparts at golden hour, and a final dinner at the country's most celebrated restaurant before a midnight flight to Hong Kong.

items.name: Barefoot Gallery
items.desc: Fabric, ready-made clothing, and gifts — the non-negotiable Galle Fort purchase, on Pedlar Street.

items.name: Galle Fort rampart walk
items.desc: A 2.5-kilometre circuit past the 17th-century Dutch Reformed Church and the lighthouse, outstanding at any hour and especially at sunset.

items.name: Gangaramaya Temple, Colombo
items.desc: An eclectic complex on Beira Lake blending Sri Lankan, Thai, Indian, and Chinese architecture, with a Bo Tree grown from the same source as Anuradhapura's, and the serene Seema Malaka meditation hall built over the water.

items.name: Galle Face Green, Colombo
items.desc: A 500-metre ocean promenade built by the British in 1859 — kite sellers, street food, and a final wide Indian Ocean horizon before the airport.

items.name: Galle lighthouse
items.desc: Sri Lanka's oldest light station, rebuilt in 1939 on a Dutch-era site at the rampart's southern tip — the classic sunrise-over-the-ramparts shot.

lodging.name: Prince of Galle
lodging.desc: A boutique property inside the fort walls, Queen Studio, with cobblestone-street access steps from Barefoot Gallery and the southern ramparts. The building itself dates to the Dutch colonial period and was restored rather than rebuilt, so original coral-stone walls and timber beams remain visible inside several rooms.

wineries.name: White-throated Kingfisher & Black-headed Ibis
wineries.desc: Common across Galle and the coastal wetlands south of Colombo.

wineries.name: Fruit bat colony, fort ramparts
wineries.desc: Large flying-fox colonies roost in the fort's old trees and stream out over the ramparts at sunset, silhouetted against the sky.

restaurants.name: Ministry of Crab
restaurants.desc: Final dinner — confirmed, Ref JH-0708-1915-0202. Giant Sri Lankan lagoon crab in the Dutch Hospital Precinct, founded by Dharshan Munidasa.

restaurants.name: Fort Bazaar Restaurant
restaurants.desc: Contemporary Sri Lankan inside the 16th-century fort walls — the signature ambulthiyal sour fish curry.

restaurants.name: Pedlar Street Bakery
restaurants.desc: Fresh-baked fish buns and short eats, popular with locals before the tourist crowds arrive.

notes.text: Place a tailoring order by 9–10am for same-day collection — choose fabric at Barefoot, then walk straight to the tailor.
CX612 departs Colombo at 00:50 on July 9 — the entire day belongs to you, with no hotel needed for the short midnight connection.
The Seema Malaka platform on Beira Lake, reflected in still water with the Colombo skyline behind, closes the loop on the Bo Tree first visited at Anuradhapura.
The fruit bats leaving the ramparts at dusk make for a striking silhouette shot with the lighthouse or church spire in frame.

days.title: Hong Kong
days.epithet: A skyline stopover between two long-haul legs
days.tagline: Dim sum, the Peak, and one last harbour crossing
days.intro: An unplanned but welcome buffer between Sri Lanka and the flight home — two easy days with no fixed agenda, in a city built vertically around one of the most photographed harbours on earth. The overnight flight lands early on July 9, leaving two full days before the evening departure on July 11.

items.name: Victoria Peak
items.desc: The classic Hong Kong skyline view, reached by the historic Peak Tram — in operation since 1888 and one of the steepest funicular railways in the world. Clearest air and best light in the hour after sunrise or before sunset.

items.name: Star Ferry, Victoria Harbour
items.desc: A seven-minute crossing between Hong Kong Island and Kowloon that has run since 1888 — still the cheapest, best harbour view in the city, especially at dusk as the skyline lights come up.

items.name: Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade
items.desc: A waterfront walk on the Kowloon side facing Hong Kong Island's tower-lined skyline directly — the Symphony of Lights show runs nightly at 8pm.

items.name: Man Mo Temple
items.desc: A 19th-century Taoist temple on Hollywood Road thick with enormous spiral incense coils hanging from the ceiling, among the oldest temples in the city.

items.name: Tai Kwun
items.desc: The former Central Police Station and colonial courthouse complex, restored into galleries, restaurants, and shops — a striking mix of heritage brick and contemporary architecture.

lodging.name: Sheraton Hong Kong Hotel & Towers
lodging.desc: On the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront in Kowloon, with harbour-facing rooms looking directly across to the Hong Kong Island skyline. The hotel has stood on this stretch of the waterfront since the early 1970s, predating most of the towers now visible from its own rooms.

wineries.name: Black kite, Victoria Harbour
wineries.desc: Black kites circle reliably over the harbour and the Peak, often close enough for a clean telephoto shot against the skyline.

restaurants.name: Tim Ho Wan
restaurants.desc: The world's most affordable Michelin-starred dim sum — baked barbecue pork buns and har gow, with a queue worth joining.

restaurants.name: Australia Dairy Company
restaurants.desc: A no-frills Hong Kong institution for scrambled eggs on toast and steamed milk pudding, in operation since the 1960s.

restaurants.name: Temple Street Night Market
restaurants.desc: Street-food stalls and seafood claypots in Kowloon after dark — a louder, more chaotic counterpoint to the quiet of Sri Lanka.

notes.text: The Airport Express train reaches Central in about 24 minutes — faster and easier than a taxi at almost any time of day.
Egg waffles (gai daan jai) from a street stall are a good anytime snack between sights.
Photograph the skyline from the Kowloon side at blue hour, just after sunset, when the building lights and the sky are balanced.
Hong Kong has more skyscrapers over 150 metres than any other city in the world, packed into one of the most mountainous urban footprints on earth.

closing.title: Homeward Bound
closing.text: Two nights in Hong Kong close out the journey gently before the final long flight home. CX876 departs Hong Kong at 4:05pm and lands in Dallas at 6:25pm the same day — the last leg of thirty-five days and six countries.

steps: Cathay Pacific CX876 departs Hong Kong 4:05pm · Premium Economy, seats 33D & 33E
Arrives Dallas–Fort Worth 6:25pm the same day, crossing the International Date Line westbound
Clear US immigration and customs at DFW — Global Entry kiosks if enrolled
Collect checked baggage and declare any items acquired in Galle or Sri Lanka
A quiet car ride home closes a twenty-fifth anniversary thirty-five days in the making

conf.detail: Sheraton Hong Kong Hotel & Towers · Jul 9–11
CX876 · 4:05pm Jul 11 · Premium Economy · Ref E3IZ4C

blocks.html: General notes for moving comfortably across six countries and five climates in thirty-five days.

rows.v: Ring Rail to Central Station, 30 min, €5.50pp
e-Visa required for US citizens, apply in advance
Maldivian Rufiyaa, USD widely accepted at resorts
Speedboat or seaplane to Vommuli, arrange with hotel in advance
ETA required for US citizens, apply online in advance
Dialog or Mobitel at the airport, roughly $5–8 for 16 days of data

items.label: Pack light
items.text: Carry-on only across 14 hotels and 28 days makes every transfer, train, and jeep safari simpler.

items.label: Laundry
items.text: India is fast and inexpensive; the Maldives is the most expensive; Sri Lanka guesthouses are reasonable and frequent.

items.label: Drivers
items.text: A private English-speaking driver-guide for the Sri Lanka leg is consistently the single best investment of that portion of the trip.

items.label: Trains
items.text: Book 1st Class Observation carriages for the hill-country legs well in advance — they sell out weeks ahead.

items.label: Health
items.text: Consult a doctor on malaria prophylaxis before travel, and carry strong insect repellent for safari mornings and evenings.

items.label: Offline maps
items.text: Download offline maps before arriving in the Cultural Triangle, where mobile data can be patchy.

blocks.html: Every confirmed flight and train of the journey, in order.

cities.name: Within the region
rows: IndiGo 6E6544 · Varanasi 10:45am → Mumbai 12:55pm · Jun 14 · PNR MCJ82A
IndiGo 6E1131 · Mumbai 10:50am → Malé 1:10pm · Jun 15 · PNR MCJ82A

cities.name: Homeward
rows: Cathay Pacific CX612 · Colombo 00:50am → Hong Kong 9:10am · Jul 9 · Business · Ref E3KDKS
Cathay Pacific CX876 · Hong Kong 4:05pm → Dallas 6:25pm · Jul 11 · Premium Economy · Ref E3IZ4C

cities.name: Vande Bharat Express 20176

Arrives Banaras Station, not Varanasi Junction — carry passports

cities.name: Hill Country Rail
rows: Kandy → Nanu Oya/Heritance Tea Factory · Jun 27 · road transfer, line suspended for repairs
Train 11005 · Ambewela 9:30am → Ella · Jun 28 · 1st Class Observation · Ref 15217051

blocks.html: A handful of words in each country's language — a little effort goes a long way.

blocks.sounds: Finns, Maldivians, and Hong Kongers generally speak excellent English — the phrases above are appreciated more as a gesture than a necessity. In Sri Lanka, “Ayubowan” with hands together is the single most useful phrase, used everywhere from monasteries to hotel lobbies.
